programs.name: Medicaid Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)

eligibility.income_limits:
Must be entitled to Medicare Part A (or eligible for premium-free Part A). Income limits are monthly and apply as follows (using most consistent WV-specific figures from 2023-2026 sources; limits adjust annually ~April 1 based on Federal Poverty Level):
- **QMB**: ≤ $1,325 single / $1,783 married (or ≤100% FPL)[2][3][7]
- **SLMB**: >QMB limit up to $1,565-$1,585 single / $2,135 married[2][3]
- **QI**: >SLMB limit up to $1,781 single / $2,400 married[2]
No variation by household size beyond single/couple; states follow federal standards but WV uses these[1][2].
eligibility.asset_limits: Total countable resources: $9,660 individual / $14,470 married couple (federal limits used in WV; consistent across QMB/SLMB/QI)[2][3]. Countable: cash, checking/savings accounts, stocks, bonds, annuities, CDs[3]. Exempt: typically primary home, one vehicle, personal belongings, burial plots (standard Medicaid exemptions apply; WV follows federal)[1][2].
other_requirements: Must be enrolled in (or eligible for) Medicare Part A and Part B[1][2][5].
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant[1].
Reside in West Virginia[2].
Not eligible for full Medicaid (QI specifically excludes full Medicaid recipients)[2][6].

benefits.value:
- **QMB**: Pays Medicare Part A premiums (if applicable), Part B premiums/deductible/coinsurance/copays; providers cannot bill beneficiary for Medicare-covered services (acts like Medigap); auto-qualifies for Extra Help (Part D low-income subsidy, ≤$12.65 copay/drug in 2026)[1][2][5].
- **SLMB**: Pays Part B premiums only[1][2].
- **QI**: Pays Part B premiums only[1][2].
Saves hundreds/thousands annually depending on utilization[1].

methods: Mail or in-person: Submit DFA-QSQ-1 form to local WV DHHR/BMS office (find via state site)[3][9].
Phone: Contact WV Bureau for Medical Services at (800) 642-8589 or local worker[3].
Online: Through WV DHHR ACCESS WV portal (dhhr.wv.gov/bcf) or BMS site[2].
application.processing_time: Effective first day of month of application if eligible; typically 45 days max but often faster[1].
application.waitlist: QI may have waitlist or funding caps (federal limit on QI slots; apply early)[1][6].
documents: Proof of income (SSI award, pay stubs, pension statements).
Proof of resources (bank statements, stock/bond values).
Medicare card (Parts A/B).
ID (SSN, birth certificate, proof of citizenship).
Proof of residency.
Household size details if married[3].

geography.restrictions: None; available statewide to eligible Medicare beneficiaries[2].
geography.regional_variations: Processing via local DHHR offices; no major benefit differences, possible minor wait time variations by county workload[2].
offices_or_providers: Local DHHR field offices (locator on dhhr.wv.gov); statewide BMS oversight[2][3]

programs.official_source: https://bms.wv.gov (WV Bureau for Medical Services); form at dhhr.wv.gov/bcf/policy/imm[3][9]
gotchas: Income/asset limits change annually (check current via BMS; figures here from 2023-2026 sources may need update)[1][2][5].
QMB providers cannot bill you, but confirm with 'QMB' status on Medicaid card; report billing violations[1][5].
QI has limited federal funding/waitlist—higher priority for earliest applicants[1][6].
Automatic Extra Help with QMB but not always SLMB/QI—apply separately if needed[1][5].
Married couples: Combined income/assets; living arrangements matter (e.g., separate households may qualify separately)[2].
Not for Medicare Advantage—applies to Original Medicare only[6].
programs.data_shape_notes: Tiered by income brackets (QMB <100% FPL, SLMB 100-120% FPL, QI 120-135% FPL); single/couple only (no larger household multipliers); federal asset caps with WV adoption; QI funding-capped

programs.name: West Virginia Aged and Disabled Waiver (ADW)

eligibility.income_limits: Must meet Medicaid Waiver financial eligibility criteria as determined by DHHR county office or SSA for SSI recipients. Specific dollar amounts not listed in sources; may qualify via Medically Needy Program if medical costs reduce income to $209/month remaining. No household size table provided.
eligibility.asset_limits: Home equity interest no greater than $730,000 (2025 limit). Exemptions: applicant lives in home or intends to return; spouse or dependent relative (child, grandchild, parent, aunt/uncle, sibling, niece/nephew) lives in home.
other_requirements: West Virginia permanent resident.

Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC): 5 functional deficits via Pre-Admission Screening (PAS) by registered nurse (e.g., ADLs like bathing/dressing, bedsores, emergency evacuation, skilled needs, medication self-administration).
Choose home/community-based services over nursing home.
Safe environment for staff.
Not solely for Medicaid eligibility or ancillary services like housekeeping/transportation.
Enrollment dependent on funded slot availability.

benefits.value: Person-centered home and community-based services (e.g., personal attendant, agency direct-care, case management, RN visits) equivalent to nursing home care, promoting independence. Specific services ordered by physician; not for household convenience.

methods: Physician completes Medical Necessity Evaluation Request (MNER) form, sends to KEPRO.
Submit MNER and KEPRO letter to local DHHR office for financial eligibility.
In-person: Local DHHR county office; Bureau of Senior Services.
Online: inRoads portal via Bureau for Medical Services DHHR.
Case Management Agency assistance available.
application.processing_time: Case manager contacts within 5 days after KEPRO notification; medical eligibility after financial confirmation (no specific overall timeline).
application.waitlist: Enrollment dependent on availability of funded ADW slot.
forms: Provider/Case Management Agency selection form (from KEPRO).
documents: Physician-completed MNER.
Proof of WV residency.
Financial documents for DHHR/SSA review.
KEPRO letter.

geography.restrictions: Permanent WV resident; discharge/transfer from nursing home in any WV county or out-of-state allowed if WV resident.
geography.regional_variations: Administered via local DHHR county offices; potential waitlist variations by funded slot availability, but no specific regional differences detailed.

gotchas: Must have exactly 5 functional deficits for NFLOC; dementia diagnosis alone insufficient.
Home equity limit $730,000 (2025); exemptions narrow.
Financial eligibility first, then medical; no medical assessment without yellow DHS-2.
Slot availability limits enrollment (waitlist risk).
Not for sole purpose of Medicaid eligibility or minor services.
Must provide safe environment for providers.
programs.data_shape_notes: Enrollment capped by funded slots; two-step process (financial then medical); physician-ordered services; person-centered with NFLOC via PAS assessment.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE) - Not specifically named in WV sources

eligibility.income_limits: No specific income limits for PACE enrollment; no financial criteria required. Free for those qualifying for Medicare and Medicaid or Medicaid only. Non-Medicaid eligible pay a flat monthly fee (amount not specified in sources). West Virginia Medicaid nursing home level generally requires income under $2,982/month for singles in 2026, but PACE does not impose this as a barrier to enrollment[7][8].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or financial criteria for PACE eligibility[2][3].
other_requirements: Live in the service area of a PACE organization (specific to West Virginia PACE provider)
Certified by West Virginia as requiring Nursing Home Level of Care (NHLOC)
Able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prepayment plan, Medicare prescription drug plan, or hospice

benefits.value: Comprehensive, coordinated care including primary medical care, in-home and outpatient therapy, adult day care and health center, scheduled transportation, assistance with meals and nutrition. All PACE-provided services covered without deductibles or co-pays once enrolled. Exact hours or dollar amounts not specified[1][2][8].

methods: Contact PACE Your Life MWV via email at info@paceyourlifemwv.com or visit their website https://paceyourlifemwv.com/ for eligibility and enrollment details[8]
General: Find local PACE via National PACE Association or Medicare tools, then contact specific provider[2][4]
application.waitlist: Possible waitlists due to capped federal/state funding, varying by program and state[5]
forms: Not specified; contact provider for enrollment process
documents: Not specified in sources; prepare proof of age, residency in service area, and medical records for NHLOC certification

geography.restrictions: Limited to service area of PACE organization in West Virginia (specific counties not detailed; provider is PACE Your Life MWV)
geography.regional_variations: Services and availability limited to areas served by specific PACE providers; West Virginia has at least one (PACE Your Life). Wait times may vary by region due to capacity[1][4][5][8]

gotchas: Not available statewide in West Virginia; must live in specific service area near PACE center
Requires Nursing Home Level of Care certification by West Virginia, which involves functional assessment
Possible waitlists due to funding caps
Private pay monthly fee if not Medicaid-eligible (amount not listed; contact provider)
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Part D, or hospice
Must be able to live safely in community with PACE help at enrollment
programs.data_shape_notes: Limited to specific service areas with one known WV provider (PACE Your Life); no income/asset test for enrollment but Medicaid status affects cost; requires state NFLOC certification; potential waitlists and regional capacity limits

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)

eligibility.income_limits: For Oct. 1, 2025 through Sept. 30, 2026, most households must meet a gross income limit of 200% of the federal poverty level: 1 person $2608/month, 2 people $3526/month, 3 people $4442/month, 4 people $5358/month, 5 people $6276/month, 6 people $7192/month, 7 people $8108/month, each additional +$916/month. Households with a member 60+ or disabled are exempt from the gross income test and only need to meet the net income test (gross minus deductions like medical expenses over $35/month for elderly/disabled, shelter costs, utilities). If gross income exceeds 200% FPL, must also meet asset test under federal rules ($4500 limit).[1][3]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limit for most households in West Virginia (home and vehicles exempt). Households with a member 60+ or disabled who exceed gross income limit must meet federal asset limit of $4500 in countable resources (e.g., bank accounts; excludes home, vehicles, retirement savings in some cases).[1]
other_requirements: West Virginia resident
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Social Security number (or applied for one)
Work requirements for able-bodied adults without dependents (ABAWDs) unless exempt (e.g., 60+, disabled, caring for child under 14 or disabled adult)
Net income test for elderly/disabled households

benefits.value: Monthly EBT card benefits for food purchases (average $188/month for older adults; exact amount based on income, household size, deductions). Benefits only for food for human consumption, seeds/plants. Certification period: 24 months for households with 60+ or disabled members (vs. 12 months for others).[3][8]

methods: Online: West Virginia DHHR benefits portal (wvpath.wv.gov)
Phone: Local county DHHR office or statewide inquiry line (1-877-716-1212)
Mail: Send application to local DHHR office
In-person: Local county DHHR office
application.processing_time: Typically 30 days; expedited for urgent cases
forms: West Virginia SNAP Application (available online at wvpath.wv.gov or local offices)
documents: Proof of income (pay stubs, Social Security statements, veterans/disability benefits)
Proof of expenses (rent/mortgage, utilities, medical costs over $35/month for 60+/disabled)
ID (driver's license, birth certificate)
Social Security numbers for household
Proof of residency

geography.regional_variations: Administered uniformly by state DHHR but apply at local county offices; no noted differences in eligibility/benefits, potential variation in processing times by county workload

gotchas: Elderly/disabled households skip gross income test but must meet net income; many miss high medical/shelter deductions that lower net income
WV expanded eligibility beyond federal standards (no asset limit for most); other sites may show stricter rules
Social Security, veterans/disability benefits count as income
Must include all who buy/prepare food together in household
24-month certification for elderly/disabled requires less frequent recertification but still report changes
programs.data_shape_notes: Benefits and gross income limits scale by household size; elderly/disabled exempt from gross income and asset tests (use net only or federal asset if over gross); statewide with county offices; high deductions for medical/shelter key for seniors

programs.name: LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program)

eligibility.income_limits: Gross monthly household income at or below 60% of West Virginia state median income (or equivalently 130% of Federal Poverty Guidelines in some sources). Example limits for FY2022: 1-person: $1,884; 2-person: $2,464; 3-person: $3,043; 4-person: $3,620; add $567 per additional person. Income from month of application; some exclusions apply (e.g., Social Security, Veterans income excluded for current period; SNAP benefits, student loans, CSEP). Household includes all at address sharing utility bill. Must be responsible for heating costs and meet vulnerability factors for regular component.
other_requirements: U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
West Virginia resident
Responsible for paying home heating/cooling bill
For crisis: immediate energy emergency (e.g., shutoff, broken heater)
Automatic eligibility if enrolled in SNAP, TANF, or SSI Code A

benefits.value: One-time direct cash payment or payment to utility/vendor for heating/cooling costs. Regular and crisis components; maximum benefits vary by income, household size, fuel type (specific max amounts not listed in sources; funding limited). Crisis for emergencies like shutoffs or fuel depletion.

methods: Online: https://www.wvpath.wv.gov/
Phone: 1-800-642-8589 (toll-free)
In-person/mail: Local DHHR/DoHS field offices, Community Action Agencies, Senior Centers
application.processing_time: Within 30 days of receipt or program open date (whichever later)
application.waitlist: No waitlist; funding limited and exhausts—apply early as benefits stop when funds run out
forms: LIEAP application (available at DHHR offices, agencies, senior centers, or DHHR website)
documents: Proof of income (month of application: pay stubs, benefit statements)
Proof of residency
Proof of citizenship/identity
Heating bill/utility statements
Social Security numbers for household

geography.regional_variations: Administered via local DHHR/DoHS field offices, Community Action Agencies, Senior Centers; potential differences in processing/wait times by county/office due to funding allocation; contact local office for specifics
offices_or_providers: DoHS field offices (all counties), Community Action Agencies, Senior Centers

gotchas: Funding fixed annually and runs out—apply early in season (heating: fall/winter; cooling: summer; crisis: emergencies only)
Household includes all sharing address/utility bill (unlike SNAP)
Income based strictly on application month; some income excluded but countable income must not exceed max
Program not year-round; closes when funds deplete—no applications accepted after
Must reapply each year; prior approval doesn't carry over
Crisis requires DHHR worker evaluation for emergency
programs.data_shape_notes: Income at 60% state median (examples scale by household size, +$567/additional); exclusions vary by period (e.g., SS/Vet income); seasonal with early application critical due to limited funding; local offices handle intake with regional processing variations
programs._raw:
{
  "name": "LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program)",
  "eligibility": {
    "age": null,
    "income_limits": "Gross monthly household income at or below 60% of West Virginia state median income (or equivalently 130% of Federal Poverty Guidelines in some sources). Example limits for FY2022: 1-person: $1,884; 2-person: $2,464; 3-person: $3,043; 4-person: $3,620; add $567 per additional person. Income from month of application; some exclusions apply (e.g., Social Security, Veterans income excluded for current period; SNAP benefits, student loans, CSEP). Household includes all at address sharing utility bill. Must be responsible for heating costs and meet vulnerability factors for regular component.",
    "asset_limits": "No asset limits mentioned.",
    "other_requirements": [
      "U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen",
      "West Virginia resident",
      "Responsible for paying home heating/cooling bill",
      "For crisis: immediate energy emergency (e.g., shutoff, broken heater)",
      "Automatic eligibility if enrolled in SNAP, TANF, or SSI Code A"
    ]
  },
  "benefits": {
    "type": "financial",
    "value": "One-time direct cash payment or payment to utility/vendor for heating/cooling costs. Regular and crisis components; maximum benefits vary by income, household size, fuel type (specific max amounts not listed in sources; funding limited). Crisis for emergencies like shutoffs or fuel depletion.",
    "varies_by": "household_size|priority_tier|region"
  },
  "application": {
    "methods": [
      "Online: https://www.wvpath.wv.gov/",
      "Phone: 1-800-642-8589 (toll-free)",
      "In-person/mail: Local DHHR/DoHS field offices, Community Action Agencies, Senior Centers"
    ],
    "processing_time": "Within 30 days of receipt or program open date (whichever later)",
    "waitlist": "No waitlist; funding limited and exhausts—apply early as benefits stop when funds run out",
    "forms": ["LIEAP application (available at DHHR offices, agencies, senior centers, or DHHR website)"],
    "documents": [
      "Proof of income (month of application: pay stubs, benefit statements)",
      "Proof of residency",
      "Proof of citizenship/identity",
      "Heating bill/utility statements",
      "Social Security numbers for household"
    ]
  },
  "geography": {
    "statewide": true,
    "restrictions": "None; available statewide",
    "regional_variations": "Administered via local DHHR/DoHS field offices, Community Action Agencies, Senior Centers; potential differences in processing/wait times by county/office due to funding allocation; contact local office for specifics",
    "offices_or_providers": ["DoHS field offices (all counties), Community Action Agencies, Senior Centers"]
  },
  "official_source": "https://bfa.wv.gov/utility-assistance/lieap or https://dhhr.wv.gov/bcf/Services/familyassistance/Pages/default.aspx",
  "gotchas": [
    "Funding fixed annually and runs out—apply early in season (heating: fall/winter; cooling: summer; crisis: emergencies only)",
    "Household includes all sharing address/utility bill (unlike SNAP)",
    "Income based strictly on application month; some income excluded but countable income must not exceed max",
    "Program not year-round; closes when funds deplete—no applications accepted after",
    "Must reapply each year; prior approval doesn't carry over",
    "Crisis requires DHHR worker evaluation for emergency"
  ],
  "data_shape_notes": "Income at 60% state median (examples scale by household size, +$567/additional); exclusions vary by period (e.g., SS/Vet income); seasonal with early application critical due to limited funding; local offices handle intake with regional processing variations"
}

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)

eligibility.income_limits: Income must be ≤200% of Federal Poverty Guidelines (effective April 8, 2025): 1: $31,300; 2: $42,300; 3: $53,300; 4: $64,300; 5: $75,300; 6: $86,300; +$11,000 per additional member. Based on gross cash receipts before taxes from prior tax year(s); excludes child support, tax refunds, scholarships, etc.[1]
other_requirements: West Virginia resident.[1]
Dwelling must meet building eligibility (structurally adaptable for weatherization).[1][2]
Priority on waitlist for households with elderly (60+), disabled, children (≤18), high energy burden, or high energy use.[1]

benefits.value: Energy conservation measures including air sealing, insulation, heat/cool tune-ups, health/safety repairs to reduce heating/cooling costs and improve home safety/comfort. No fixed dollar amount or hours; services based on audit and funding availability.[1][2][7]

methods: Contact local Community Action Agency (CAA) serving your county (in-person, phone, or mail application).[1][2][7]
Online application available statewide.[7]
Specific: MountainHeart (Wyoming/Fayette): Call 304-682-8271 ext. 101 (Angela Beaver) or ext. 117 (Gerald Brown), or visit 33 MountainHeart Lane, Matheny WV 24860.[7]
CASEWV serves certain areas (check casewv.org).[3]
application.processing_time: Eligibility determination after application (income verification time-consuming); then placed on waitlist for audit/services. No fixed timeline specified.[2]
application.waitlist: Yes, all eligible applicants placed on waitlist; prioritized by points for high energy burden/use, elderly (60+), disabled, children.[1]
forms: Standard application form at local CAA (takes ~20 minutes); no specific form name/number statewide.[2]
documents: Proof of all gross income for prior year (e.g., tax returns, pay stubs, etc.).[1][2]
Household details: number of people, home construction date, utility providers/account numbers.[2]
If renting: Landlord permission.[2]

geography.restrictions: Available in all WV counties via local CAAs.[1][2][6]
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local Community Action Agencies/providers per county/region (e.g., CASEWV, MountainHeart, Southwestern Community Action Council); may have different contacts, offices, wait times.[2][3][7][9]
offices_or_providers: Local CAAs; examples: wvcad.org for statewide, casewv.org, mthtwv.com (MountainHeart), scacwv.org.[1][3][7][9]

gotchas: Income based on prior full tax year gross cash income (verification time-consuming).[1][2]
Dwelling must qualify structurally; not all homes eligible.[1]
Renters need landlord permission first.[2]
Waitlist prioritization favors elderly/disabled/children/high energy needs—not first-come.[1]
Funding limited (e.g., $4.3M for 2024-2025); services depend on federal appropriations.[1]
Separate from DHHR Repair/Replacement Program (60% SMI, for heating repairs).[3]
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered via county-specific Community Action Agencies; waitlist with priority tiers (elderly/disabled/children/high energy); income table scales by household size at 200% FPG; building eligibility required.

programs.name: State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; prioritizes people with limited incomes but open to all Medicare beneficiaries[1][2]
other_requirements: Must be a Medicare beneficiary (age 65+ or under 65 with disabilities), their families, or caregivers[1][2][3]

benefits.value: Free one-on-one confidential counseling, education, and assistance on Medicare Parts A/B/D, Medigap, Medicare Advantage, long-term care insurance, Medicare Savings Programs (QMB/SLMB/QI), prescription drug assistance, Medicaid, appeals, fraud prevention (via SMP), referrals, printed materials, group presentations, and help applying for cost-saving programs like Extra Help[1][2][3][4]

methods: Website: https://www.wvship.org (for info, comparisons, appointments)[3][4][9]

In-person: At WV Bureau of Senior Services (1900 Kanawha Blvd East, 3rd Floor Town Center Mall, Charleston WV 25305) or many county senior centers and Aging & Disability Resource Centers statewide[3][4][5]
Email: rebecca.a.gouty@wv.gov[4]
application.processing_time: No formal application or processing; services provided via appointment upon contact[2][3]
forms: No specific forms required; counselors assist with Medicare-related forms during sessions[1]
documents: Medicare card, current insurance details, prescription list, income info if applying for savings programs (counselors help gather)[2]

geography.restrictions: None; available statewide to WV Medicare beneficiaries[1][3][4]
geography.regional_variations: Counselors located at county senior centers and Aging & Disability Resource Centers; no noted differences in services, wait times, or providers by region[3][5]
offices_or_providers: Main office: 1900 Kanawha Blvd East, Charleston WV 25305[4][5]

gotchas: Not a healthcare or financial aid program—provides counseling only, not direct payments or medical care[1][2]
Free but requires contacting for appointment; no walk-ins guaranteed outside main office[3]
Often confused with Medicare Savings Programs (which SHIP helps apply for); SHIP itself has no eligibility barriers[1]
Services via local county centers may vary in availability by location[5]
programs.data_shape_notes: no income/asset test; counseling only, delivered via statewide network of county senior centers; prioritizes limited-income Medicare beneficiaries but open to all[1][3][5]

programs.name: Meals on Wheels
programs._raw:
I cannot provide the comprehensive JSON guide you've requested because the search results do not contain the specific, detailed information needed to complete it accurately.

Here's what the search results **do** provide about West Virginia Meals on Wheels:

**What I can confirm:**
- **Eligibility:** Age 60+, homebound or living alone, and physically or mentally unable to obtain or prepare food[6]. Spouses and disabled dependents of eligible participants may also qualify, regardless of age[6].
- **Services:** Home-delivered meals meeting one-third of recommended daily allowance for vitamins and minerals[7].
- **Application route:** Contact your local Area Agency on Aging[2].
- **General process:** Initial assessment covering age, health, mobility, meal preparation ability, dietary restrictions, and medical conditions[2].

**What the search results do NOT contain:**
- Specific income or asset limits (dollar amounts, household tables)
- Specific benefit values (meals per week, dollar amounts)
- Actual phone numbers, websites, or form names for West Virginia
- Processing timelines specific to West Virginia
- County-by-county or regional variations within West Virginia
- Specific offices or providers by region
- Waitlist information
- Specific documents required

**Why this matters:** The search results include general national Meals on Wheels information and one West Virginia fact sheet (from 2018-2019) that only covers demographic data, not program specifics. To build an accurate guide, you would need to contact West Virginia's Area Agencies on Aging directly or access the state's official Older Americans Act program documentation, which is not included in these results.

I recommend contacting your state's Department of Health and Human Resources or the West Virginia Commission on Aging for the specific details needed.

programs.name: National Family Caregiver Support Program
programs._raw:
I cannot provide the comprehensive JSON guide you've requested because the search results do not contain the specific, detailed information needed to populate most of the fields accurately.

Here's what the search results actually cover about the **National Family Caregiver Support Program (NFCSP)** in West Virginia:

**What IS in the search results:**

The NFCSP eligibility criteria are defined at the federal level[4]: the program serves adult family members or informal caregivers providing care to individuals 60 years of age and older, or caregivers of any age providing care to individuals with Alzheimer's disease and related disorders, or older relatives (age 55+) caring for children under 18.

West Virginia has a related non-Medicaid program called the **Lighthouse Program**[8], which provides personal care, mobility, nutrition, and housekeeping services and allows certain family members to be hired and compensated as caregivers—but the search results don't specify eligibility criteria, income limits, asset limits, benefit amounts, application procedures, processing times, or regional variations.

**What is NOT in the search results:**

- Specific income or asset limits for West Virginia
- Dollar amounts or hours of service provided
- Processing timelines or waitlist information
- Specific application forms, phone numbers, or websites for the NFCSP in West Virginia
- Documents required
- Regional variations within West Virginia
- How the program differs from similar offerings

**Why this matters:**

The search results conflate multiple programs (NFCSP, Lighthouse, PGCSS, PCAFC) without clearly delineating which is which or providing the operational details you need. To build an accurate guide, you would need to contact the West Virginia Department of Aging directly or access the official NFCSP state plan documentation, which is not included in these results.

I recommend contacting the **Caregiver Support Line at 855-260-3274**[2] or your local Area Agency on Aging for the specific details required for your guide.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)

eligibility.income_limits: Family income no more than 125% of the federal poverty level. Exact dollar amounts vary annually and by household size; families must contact local provider to confirm current thresholds based on federal poverty guidelines[3][4].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in available sources[3][4].
other_requirements: Unemployed
Low-income (≤125% FPL)

Priority given to veterans and qualified spouses, individuals over 65, those with disabilities, low literacy or limited English proficiency, rural residents, homeless or at-risk, low employment prospects, or those who failed to find employment via American Job Centers[3]

benefits.value: Part-time community service job training (average 20 hours/week) at public/nonprofit agencies (e.g., schools, hospitals, senior centers); paid highest of federal, state, or local minimum wage; skill development, job placement assistance, annual physical exam, support services (Social Security info, tax requirements, nutrition, grooming); bridge to unsubsidized employment[1][3][4].

methods: Phone: Preston County Senior Citizens, Inc. at (304) 329-0464 for Boone, Clay, Doddridge, Harrison, Kanawha, Mercer, Monongalia, Preston, Putnam, Raleigh, Taylor Counties[1]

Contact Southwestern Community Action Council for Barbour, Braxton, Cabell, Fayette, Greenbrier, Kanawha, Lincoln, Logan, McDowell, Mercer, Mingo Counties (website: https://scacwv.org/scsep, phone not specified in results)[6]
Human Resource Development Foundation (HRDF) for other areas (website: https://www.hrdfwv.org/senior-works.html, phone not specified)[2]
General locator: https://my.aarpfoundation.org/locator/scsep/[8]
application.processing_time: Most participants trained for about 6 months before job placement assistance[4]
application.waitlist: Not specified; may vary by region and funding[4]
documents: Proof of age, income verification, unemployment status (contact local provider for exact list)

geography.restrictions: Served by multiple grantees covering specific counties; not all counties listed in results (e.g., Preston County Senior Citizens covers 11 counties, Southwestern covers 10 overlapping in Kanawha/Mercer)[1][6]
geography.regional_variations: Administered by different nonprofits (e.g., Preston County Senior Citizens, Inc., Southwestern Community Action Council, HRDF); availability, wait times, and host agencies may differ by provider and county[1][2][6]
offices_or_providers: Preston County Senior Citizens, Inc. (Boone, Clay, Doddridge, Harrison, Kanawha, Mercer, Monongalia, Preston, Putnam, Raleigh, Taylor)[1]
Southwestern Community Action Council (Barbour, Braxton, Cabell, Fayette, Greenbrier, Kanawha, Lincoln, Logan, McDowell, Mercer, Mingo)[6]
Human Resource Development Foundation (statewide vision, specific counties unclear)[2]

gotchas: Not available uniformly statewide; must confirm county-specific provider as coverage overlaps and gaps may exist[1][6]
Income limit is 125% FPL for family size—requires verification of current federal poverty guidelines[3][4]
Priority enrollment tiers may delay access for non-priority applicants[3]
Temporary training (avg. 20 hrs/wk, ~6 months) aimed at unsubsidized jobs, not long-term employment[3][4]
Funded by DOL grants to nonprofits; subject to federal funding changes/delays[4][5]
programs.data_shape_notes: Multiple regional grantees with county-specific coverage and contact points; income test at 125% FPL; priority tiers affect access; part-time training only via nonprofit/public host sites

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors (e.g., Lighthouse Program elements)

eligibility.income_limits: Financially eligible based on the LIGHTHOUSE Sliding Fee Schedule. Income may be determined by voluntary disclosure or agency estimation; no strict means test required. Specific dollar amounts not listed in sources, but designed for seniors disqualified from Medicaid by income or assets.[5][1]
eligibility.asset_limits: Assets that disqualify from Medicaid services exclude eligibility for Medicaid but do not impose separate asset limits for Lighthouse; focuses on those over Medicaid thresholds.[1][5]
other_requirements: Medically eligible based on functional evaluation by Aging & Family Services Registered Nurse. Two needs must be identified under Activities of Daily Living (ADL) requiring 'Much Assistance' or 'Cannot Perform'.[5]
Functional needs in the home (personal care, mobility, nutrition, housekeeping).[1][5]

benefits.value: In-home support in four areas: Personal Care (grooming, bathing, dressing, toileting); Mobility (transferring, walking); Nutrition (meal preparation, eating, grocery/pharmacy shopping); Environment (light housekeeping, bed making/changing, client laundry). Environmental services limited to no more than 1/3 of monthly hours. Up to 60 hours per month based on RN assessment and plan of care, provided by In-Home Care Technician.[5][1]

methods: Contact local senior center or provider in each county (e.g., Nicholas Community Action Partnership for Nicholas County).[1]
Call Aging & Family Services at 304-788-5467.[5]
County senior aging programs employ caregivers and handle intake.[1]
application.waitlist: Based on need and availability of caregivers; potential waitlist implied but not detailed.[1]
forms: BoSS Intake Form (BIF) for ADL evaluation.[5]
documents: Income information (voluntary disclosure or agency estimation).[5]
Documentation for functional needs assessment by RN.[5]

geography.restrictions: Available in each county through local senior centers.[1][5]
geography.regional_variations: Administered through county senior centers and local providers (e.g., Nicholas Community Action Partnership, Raleigh Seniors); services provided by local In-Home Care Technicians; potential variations in availability and wait times by county.[1][8]

gotchas: Not legal aid; provides in-home care services (personal care, etc.), not attorney services—user query mixes with West Virginia Senior Legal Aid (WVSLA), which is separate (age 60+, no income limits, call 1-800-229-5068).[2][4][5]
Up to 60 hours/month only, based on RN assessment, caregiver availability, and sliding fee (may involve client cost-sharing).[1][5]
Environmental services capped at 1/3 of hours; must have 2+ ADL needs at 'Much Assistance' or 'Cannot Perform' level.[5]
Designed specifically for those over Medicaid income/asset limits.[1][5]
programs.data_shape_notes: Statewide via 55 county senior centers; sliding fee scale (no fixed income table published); medically tiered by RN ADL assessment; hours capped at 60/month with service area limits; not true legal aid but in-home care for seniors ineligible for Medicaid.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; services are free and available to any resident, family, or concerned party regardless of income.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits; no financial eligibility requirements apply.
other_requirements: Must be a resident of a long-term care facility in West Virginia (nursing homes, assisted living facilities, residential care communities, or legally unlicensed homes), or a family member, caretaker, or concerned friend acting on behalf of such a resident.

benefits.value: Investigating and resolving complaints about health, safety, welfare, and rights; routine unannounced facility visits to monitor care; providing information and referral services on long-term care options; representing residents to government agencies; educating on rights; training volunteers. All services are free and confidential.

methods: Phone: Toll-free 1-800-834-0598 (with regional extensions, e.g., ext. 3912 for Director Ed Hopple, ext. 2812 for Martinsburg); Regional offices (e.g., 304-263-8871 ext. 2812 for Martinsburg, 304-449-4755 for Director); In-person at regional offices (e.g., 301 W. Burke St., Suite B, Martinsburg, WV 25401)
application.processing_time: Complaints are investigated promptly; no fixed timeline specified, but ombudsmen address issues as they arise including on own initiative.
forms: No specific application form required; contact ombudsman directly to file a complaint or request assistance.
documents: No documents needed to initiate contact or file a complaint; provide details of the issue verbally or in writing.

geography.restrictions: Limited to residents in long-term care facilities across West Virginia; state divided into 9 regions.
geography.regional_variations: Program operates through 9 regional ombudsmen with specific counties served and local offices; services consistent statewide but delivered by regional advocates (e.g., Brenda Alvarado serves Clay, Fayette, Nicholas, Kanawha; Mary Nicholas in Martinsburg serves Berkeley, Grant, Hampshire, Hardy, Jefferson, Mineral, Morgan, Pendleton, Preston, Tucker).
offices_or_providers: Legal Aid of West Virginia operates the program; regional examples: Martinsburg (301 W. Burke St., Suite B), Charleston (922 Quarrier Street, 4th Floor), Morgantown (165 Scott Ave., Ste 209), Huntington (418 8th Street, Second Floor).

gotchas: This is not a direct service provider (e.g., no healthcare, housing, or financial aid); it's purely advocacy and complaint resolution. Not for general eligibility determination but for issues in facilities. Ombudsman staff are non-attorneys. Access requires facility residency or acting on behalf of one. No entry to certain facility areas without resident consent. For health facility complaints outside ombudsman scope, use OHFLAC at (304) 558-0050.
programs.data_shape_notes: no income test; advocacy-only for long-term care facility residents; delivered via 9 regional non-attorney ombudsmen under Legal Aid WV; open to residents, families, friends without eligibility barriers.

programs.name: Lighthouse Program

eligibility.income_limits: Financially eligible based on a sliding fee scale determined by the individual's (and spouse's, if applicable) annual income. No fixed dollar amounts or household size table specified in sources; fee for services required per state cost share schedule. Means tests not required—income by voluntary disclosure or agency estimation.[2][3][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset test or limits mentioned. Designed for seniors whose income or assets disqualify them from Medicaid, but program itself has no financial disqualification beyond sliding fee scale.[2][4]
other_requirements: West Virginia resident[3][4][7]

Medically eligible based on functional evaluation by the local senior program's Registered Nurse (RN). Typically requires two needs in Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) rated as 'Much Assistance' or 'Cannot Perform' on BoSS Intake Form (BIF).[1][2][3][5][6]

benefits.value: In-home supportive services in four areas: 1) Personal Care (grooming, bathing, dressing, toileting); 2) Mobility (transferring, walking); 3) Nutrition (meal preparation, eating, grocery/pharmacy shopping); 4) Environment (light housekeeping, making/changing bed, client's laundry—limited to no more than 1/3 of monthly hours). Up to 60 hours per month, based on need, RN-assessed plan of care, and caregiver availability. Provided by in-home care technicians employed by local senior programs.[2][3][4][5]

methods: Contact your local county senior center/program (one in each WV county) for RN evaluation and intake[3][4]
Call West Virginia Bureau of Senior Services toll-free: 1-877-987-3646[3]

County-specific examples: Mason County Action Group (304) 675-2369[1]; Preston County Senior Citizens (contact via site)[2]; Nicholas Community Action Partnership (contact via site)[4]; Aging & Family Services (contact via site)[5]
application.waitlist: Services based on availability of caregivers; potential wait implied but not detailed[3][4]
forms: BoSS Intake Form (BIF) for ADL assessment[5]
documents: Income information for sliding fee determination (voluntary disclosure)[2][5]
No other specific documents listed; prepare for RN functional evaluation

geography.restrictions: Available in every county of West Virginia; services provided in-home within the county[3][4][5]
geography.regional_variations: Administered by over 140 local senior centers/programs (one per county); services uniform but delivered by different local providers with potential variations in wait times or availability based on local caregivers. Examples: Mason County Action Group, Preston County Senior Citizens, Nicholas CAP, Aging & Family Services, Raleigh Seniors.[1][2][4][5][6]
offices_or_providers: Local county senior centers/programs (contact WV Bureau of Senior Services for your county)[3]

programs.official_source: http://www.wvseniorservices.gov (West Virginia Bureau of Senior Services)[3]
gotchas: Must pay a sliding fee based on income—not free; fee determined by state schedule[1][2][3]
Limited to 60 hours/month max, based on need and caregiver availability—may not cover full needs[2][3][4]
Environmental services (housekeeping) capped at 1/3 of monthly hours[5]
Targeted at those ineligible for Medicaid/other programs; not a Medicaid waiver[4][5][6]
Medically eligible only after RN assessment—two ADL needs typically required[5]
programs.data_shape_notes: State-funded program delivered uniformly via 140+ local county senior centers; no strict income/asset caps but sliding fee scale; medically tiered by RN-assessed ADL needs; hours capped at 60/month with environmental services limited to 1/3

programs.name: FAIR (Family Alzheimer's In-Home Respite)

eligibility.income_limits: No information found in search results; program appears unrelated to Alzheimer's respite services.
eligibility.asset_limits: No information found; no details on assets, exemptions, or counts.
other_requirements: Search results do not describe a Family Alzheimer's In-Home Respite program in West Virginia. All results refer to the West Virginia FAIR Plan, a property insurance program for high-risk homes unable to obtain voluntary market coverage.[1][3][5]

benefits.value: Essential property insurance: fire/lightning, wind/hail, aircraft/vehicles, smoke/explosion (up to $200,000 dwellings, $500,000 commercial). No liability or vandalism on habitational properties.[1][6]

methods: Through licensed property insurance agent/broker only. Submit WVFP 4/ACORD 64 form with photos (front/rear views). Electronic option available. Mail to FAIR Plan office.[4]
application.processing_time: Inspection ordered post-approval; timeline not specified.[1]
forms: WVFP 4/ACORD 64 (application), PDWV-25/ACORD 65 (supplementary if vacant/unoccupied or specific yes answers).[4]
documents: Two photos of structure (front/rear), completed/signed application by applicant and agent.[4]

geography.restrictions: Statewide for West Virginia properties; coal mine subsidence mandatory outside specific counties (Berkley, Cabell, etc.) unless waived.[5][6]
geography.regional_variations: Coverage type may depend on location; coal subsidence rules vary by county.[6]
offices_or_providers: Admin office: 190 N. Independence Mall West, Suite 301, Philadelphia, PA 19106 (serves WV).[5]

gotchas: Must first attempt voluntary market insurance; requires licensed agent (no direct public application); incomplete apps rejected; no liability coverage; higher cost/less coverage than private insurance.[1][3][4]
Not a healthcare/Alzheimer's program—user query mismatches all sources.
programs.data_shape_notes: No Alzheimer's respite program found; all data pertains to property insurance FAIR Plan. Eligibility based on insurability/fire standards, not income/age/health. Agent-mediated applications only.

programs.name: West Virginia Senior Legal Aid

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits for basic eligibility. However, higher levels of service (legal advice and legal advocacy) are prioritized for seniors who are low-income, minority, disabled, very rural, LGBT, or very elderly.[3]
other_requirements: Must be a West Virginia resident[1]
Legal issue must be civil in nature (not criminal)[1]
The senior age 60+ must be the one who calls to request services, not a family member or representative calling on their behalf[6]

benefits.value: Free civil legal services. Specific services include legal advice, legal advocacy, and representation in court proceedings. No fee charged for any services.[4]
benefits.service_details: Basic eligibility (age 60+, WV resident) qualifies for civil legal services. Enhanced service levels (legal advice and advocacy) are provided to seniors who are low-income, minority, disabled, very rural, LGBT, or very elderly.[3]

methods: Email: info@seniorlegalaid.org (described as 'the most efficient method of initial contact')[1]
Mail or in-person: Contact through phone/email for specific office locations
application.forms: No specific form names or numbers provided in available sources.
documents: Full name[1]
Telephone number[1]
Email address (if available)[1]
Brief description of legal issue and/or assistance requested[1]

geography.restrictions: Statewide service for West Virginia residents age 60+.[1][3]
geography.offices_or_providers: Specific office locations not provided in available sources. Contact via phone or email to identify local office.

gotchas: The senior age 60+ must personally call to request services—family members cannot call on their behalf.[6]
This program handles civil legal matters only; it does not handle criminal cases, accidents, or personal injury issues.[1]

No income limits exist for basic eligibility, but higher service levels are prioritized for low-income seniors and other vulnerable populations.[3] Families should not assume their income disqualifies them.

This is a separate program from Legal Aid of West Virginia (which serves low-income residents of all ages). Seniors age 60+ should contact West Virginia Senior Legal Aid specifically, not the general Legal Aid program.[3][6]
Specific dollar amounts, hours of service, and processing timelines are not publicly detailed in available sources—families should ask during initial contact.
programs.data_shape_notes: This program is distinguished by its age-based eligibility (60+) with no income limits for basic access, but tiered service levels based on vulnerability factors. It is statewide and serves only civil legal matters. Unlike Legal Aid of West Virginia (which requires low-income status), Senior Legal Aid prioritizes seniors based on age and vulnerability rather than income alone. Specific operational details (processing times, regional offices, service capacity) are not documented in publicly available sources.

## Key Takeaways for Families

**Eligibility is straightforward but has one critical requirement:** Your loved one must be 60 or older, a West Virginia resident, and have a civil legal issue (not criminal).[1][3] There are no income limits—this is fundamentally different from Legal Aid of West Virginia, which requires low-income status.[3]

**The service tier matters:** While anyone 60+ qualifies for services, seniors who are low-income, minority, disabled, very rural, LGBT, or very elderly receive prioritized access to legal advice and advocacy.[3] This means a higher-income senior will still receive services, but may receive more limited assistance.

**Application requires the senior to call personally:** This is the critical "gotcha"—the person age 60+ must make the initial contact themselves; family members cannot call on their behalf.[6] Call **1-800-229-5068** or email **info@seniorlegalaid.org** with the senior's name, phone number, email, and a brief description of the legal issue.[1]

**What types of cases they handle:** West Virginia Senior Legal Aid provides free civil legal services, but the search results do not specify which civil matters they prioritize (unlike Legal Aid of West Virginia, which explicitly lists domestic violence, housing, benefits, bankruptcy, and custody).[1] Families should ask during the intake call.

**What's missing from public information:** Processing times, specific office locations, waitlist status, and detailed service descriptions are not publicly available. Families will need to contact the program directly for these details.
